title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report — the Grafana data showing a consistent daily memory climb is really helpful.

To help narrow down where the leak might be, could you tell me which parts of TaskFlow are getting the heaviest use from your 200 users?

1. **Mostly the web UI** — users browsing/updating tasks through the browser
2. **Mostly the API** — integrations, automations, or scripts hitting the REST API
3. **Heavy use of real-time features** — WebSocket-based notifications, live board updates, etc.
4. **A mix of everything** roughly equally
5. **Something else**

This will help determine which subsystem is most likely accumulating memory.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It's mostly option 4 — a mix of everything. Our users are pretty active across the board: they use the web UI throughout the day, we have some integrations hitting the API, and we do use the real-time notification features. That said, I did notice that the v2.3 upgrade we did about a week ago mentioned 'improved real-time notifications' in the changelog, and the memory issues started right after that upgrade. We were on v2.2 before with no problems. Not sure if that's related but the timing lines up perfectly.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That timing with the v2.3 upgrade is a really strong clue — it almost certainly points to the real-time notification changes introducing a memory leak (e.g., WebSocket connections or event listeners not being cleaned up properly).

Before I write this up for the dev team, one question that would help them pinpoint the fix faster:

Do you see the **number of active WebSocket connections** climbing throughout the day on your Grafana dashboard (or in your server metrics), even as the number of logged-in users stays roughly the same?

1. **Yes** — connection count grows well beyond the ~200 user count
2. **No** — connections stay proportional to active users, but memory still climbs
3. **Not sure / we don't track that metric**

This will tell us whether the leak is in connection handling itself or somewhere deeper in the notification event pipeline.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I'm not 100% sure — I don't think we have a specific WebSocket connection count panel in our Grafana setup. What I can tell you is that the active user count stays pretty steady throughout the day (around 150-200 depending on the time), and the memory just climbs linearly regardless. So I'd say option 3, we don't track that specific metric. But if it helps, the memory growth seems to correlate more with overall request volume than with how many users are logged in at any given moment. Like, during our busiest hours the climb is steeper.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak introduced in v2.3: real-time notification subsystem accumulates memory proportional to request volume","problem":"After upgrading from v2.2 to v2.3, the TaskFlow server leaks memory throughout the day, climbing from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. Page loads degrade to 10+ seconds and API calls time out, requiring a daily restart. The leak correlates with overall request volume rather than active user count.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The v2.3 'improved real-time notifications' changes likely introduced a per-request resource leak — most probably event listeners, notification handlers, or subscription objects that are registered on each incoming request but never cleaned up. The correlation with request volume (not user count) points to something accumulating per-request rather than per-connection.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 with real-time notifications enabled","Simulate sustained traffic from ~200 users across web UI, API, and real-time features","Monitor memory usage over several hours","Observe linear memory growth correlated with request volume","Compare against the same workload on v2.2 to confirm no leak"],"environment":"Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2 approximately one week ago), ~200 active users, mix of web UI, API, and real-time notification usage","severity":"high","impact":"All ~200 users experience progressively degrading performance throughout the day, with the application becoming effectively unusable by late afternoon. Requires daily manual restarts to maintain service.","recommended_fix":"Diff the real-time notification subsystem between v2.2 and v2.3. Look specifically for: (1) event listeners or notification handlers registered per-request that are never removed, (2) subscription objects or callback references accumulating in a global/module-level collection, (3) missing cleanup in WebSocket disconnect or request-end hooks. A heap snapshot comparison between startup and after sustained load should pinpoint the leaking object type.","proposed_test_case":"Run a load test simulating 8 hours of typical usage (~200 concurrent users, mixed workload) against v2.3. Assert that memory usage stays within a bounded range (e.g., does not exceed 2x startup memory). The same test against v2.2 should serve as the passing baseline.","information_gaps":["Exact WebSocket connection count over time (reporter does not track this metric)","Whether disabling real-time notifications alone stops the leak (not tested by reporter)","Heap dump or profiler output identifying the specific leaking object type"]}
